en.extract:
The canary flyrobin (Devioeca papuana), also known as the Papuan flycatcher, canary robin, canary flycatcher, or montane flycatcher, is a species of bird in the family Petroicidae.
It is found in New Guinea.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forests with elevations from 1,100–3,500 m (3,609–11,483 ft). Currently, its population is believed to be stable.
The canary flyrobin was described by the German ornithologist, Adolf Bernhard Meyer, in 1875, from a specimen collected in the Arfak Mountains on the island of New Guinea. He coined the binomial name Microeca papuana. It was moved to the resurrected genus Devioeca, based on the results of a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2011. The genus Devioeca was originally introduced by the Australian ornithologist Gregory Mathews in 1925.

The '''canary flyrobin''' ('''''Devioeca papuana'''''), also known as the '''Papuan flycatcher''', '''canary robin''', '''canary flycatcher''', or '''montane flycatcher''', is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Petroicidae]].
It is found in [[New Guinea]].
Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s with elevations from 1,100–3,500 m (3,609–11,483 ft). Currently, its population is believed to be stable.<ref>{{Cite web|last=International)|first=BirdLife International (BirdLife|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Devioeca papuana|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|access-date=2021-05-11|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref>

The canary flyrobin was described by the German ornithologist, [[Adolf Bernhard Meyer]], in 1875, from a specimen collected in the [[Arfak Mountains]] on the island of [[New Guinea]]. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Microeca papuana''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Meyer | first=Adolf Bernhard | author-link=Adolf Bernhard Meyer | year=1875 | title=Neu im Jahre 1873 vom ihm entedeckte Vögel von Neu-Guinea und der Insel Jobi im Norden Neu-Guinea's | language=German | journal=Sitzungsberichte der Naturwissenschaftlichen Gesellschaft Isis in Dresden | pages=74–76 [75] | url=https://digital.slub-dresden.de/werkansicht/dlf/14826/381/0/ }}<!--not available from BHL--></ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=561 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484262 }}</ref> It was moved to the resurrected genus ''Devioeca'', based on the results of a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2011.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Christidis | first1=L. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Rowe | first3=D. | last4=Boles | first4=W.E. | last5=Norman | first5=J.A. | year=2011 | title=Mitochondrial and nuclear DNA phylogenies reveal a complex evolutionary history in the Australasian robins (Passeriformes: Petroicidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=61 | issue=3 | pages=726–738 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.08.014 | pmid=21867765 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=Australasian robins, rockfowl, rockjumpers, Rail-babbler | work=World Bird List Version 9.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/au_robins/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=18 June 2019 }}</ref> The genus ''Devioeca'' was originally introduced by the Australian ornithologist [[Gregory Mathews]] in 1925.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Mathews | first=Gregory | author-link=Gregory Mathews | year=1925 | title=''Devioeca'' | journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club | volume=45 | page=93 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40498938 }}</ref>
